one_line_takeaway: A modular open-source framework unifying 19 attacks, 29 defenses, and 19 evaluators over a 342-prompt/12-category test set, benchmarking 14 LLMs and showing Claude-3.5 most robust (ASR 0.11) vs open models near 0.50, while exposing that ASR itself swings by up to 0.34 depending on which evaluator judges 'success'.
key_finding: Under aggregated ASR, closed models are far more robust than open ones: Claude-3.5 Sonnet is strongest (avg ASR 0.11, lowest std 0.11), followed by OpenAI-o1 (0.21) and GPT-5 (0.28); Grok-3 is the weakest closed model (0.42). Open-source models are markedly more vulnerable (Vicuna-7B 0.51, Deepseek-R1 0.50 avg), with Llama-3.1-Instruct the most robust open model (0.31). The authors' Morpheus attack is the single strongest method across the board (e.g. 0.88 on GPT-4.1 and GPT-4.1-mini, 0.80 Grok-3, 0.77 Vicuna-7B), including against Claude-3.5 (0.40) where most attacks fail. Crucially, ASR depends heavily on which evaluator is used: for the same Vicuna-7B attack outputs, evaluators disagree sharply (PAIR-attack ASR has std 0.34 across evaluators; ShieldGemma and the authors' RADAR consistently score attacks far lower than LLM-judge evaluators), which the paper frames as evidence the field lacks a standardized evaluation.
core_contribution: TeleAI-Safety is a unified, modular framework-plus-benchmark for evaluating LLM robustness to jailbreak attacks. Its own contributions are: (1) an integrated open-source system that co-locates 19 attack methods, 29 defense methods, and 19 evaluation methods behind standardized YAML-configurable interfaces so attack/defense/evaluation combinations can be composed reproducibly; (2) a curated 342-sample harmful-prompt test set spanning 12 risk categories, built by filtering existing benchmarks (GuidedBench, HarmBench, JailbreakBench, AdvBench) under refusal/intent/authenticity criteria and a risk taxonomy grounded in Chinese national standards (GB/T 45654-2025) cross-referenced with Aegis 2.0, MITRE ATLAS, and OWASP Top 10 for LLMs; and (3) a benchmark run applying this suite across 14 target models (9 closed-source, 5 open-source) reporting Attack Success Rate (ASR). The framework also folds in two methods the authors developed in prior work (cited, not newly introduced here): Morpheus [7], a self-evolving metacognitive multi-round attack agent, and RADAR [8], a multi-agent-debate evaluator. The central positioning claim is balanced integration of all three components plus a standardized dataset, which the paper argues prior benchmarks (JailJudge, EasyJailbreak, HarmBench, AISafetyLab, PandaGuard) each only partially provide.
threat_model: Adversary crafts adversarial prompts x_adv to make a safety-aligned LLM M produce policy-violating (harmful) output that also semantically satisfies a target harmful instruction h, i.e. jailbreaking past built-in safety guardrails. Attacker capability is stratified by model access: white-box (full parameters/gradients, e.g. GCG, AutoDAN, LAA, SCAV, Random Search), gray-box (partial info such as output logits), and black-box (API-only prompt manipulation, e.g. TAP, PAIR, Cipher, DeepInception, ArtPrompt, Past Tense, Multilingual, Morpheus). Defenses span external interface-level guardrails (input filtering like PPL/PromptGuard, output filtering like Self-Defense/Aligner) and internal inference-time (SafeDecoding, GradSafe, RAIN) and training-time (safety fine-tuning, adversarial training, model editing) mechanisms.
limitations: Evaluation depth is uneven: the full 14-model sweep only covers attacks (Tables 2-3), while the attack-defense grid and the evaluator-sensitivity study are run on a single old model (Vicuna-7B), so defense and evaluator conclusions do not generalize across the model set. Two of the headline 'self-developed' methods (Morpheus, RADAR) are the authors' own prior cited works integrated into the suite rather than new methods introduced in this paper, and Morpheus being reported as the top attack while RADAR is offered as a favored evaluator creates an obvious self-comparison bias. ASR is the only safety metric and depends on the chosen evaluator (which the paper itself shows can swing results by up to 0.34), and utility/safety trade-offs are asserted but not quantified in the paper. The dataset is only 342 samples with a deliberately non-uniform category distribution, and its taxonomy is anchored to Chinese regulatory standards, which may bias category coverage. It is a benchmark/integration paper: no new defense is proposed and no attack mechanism is newly invented here.

datasets_used: TeleAI-Safety dataset: 342 manually curated harmful prompts across 12 risk categories (self-constructed test set)
Source pools for curation: GuidedBench, HarmBench, JailbreakBench, AdvBench

12 risk categories: Political Risk, Medical Risk, Personal Safety Risk, Commercial Violations, Information Theft, Pornographic Content, Insults and Discrimination, Content Fabrication, Violence and Terrorism, Intellectual Property Protection, Harm to Minors, Cybersecurity
quantitative_results: Framework scope: 19 attack methods, 29 defense methods, 19 evaluation methods; 14 target models (9 closed-source, 5 open-source)
Black-box avg ASR: Claude-3.5 0.11 (std 0.11), OpenAI-o1 0.21, GPT-5 0.28, GPT-4.1 0.36, GPT-4o-mini 0.37, GPT-4.1-mini 0.38, Grok-3-mini 0.38, Gemini-2.5 0.38, Grok-3 0.42
White-box avg ASR: Vicuna-7B 0.51, Deepseek-R1 0.50, Qwen-2.5-Instruct 0.39, Qwen-1.5-Chat 0.37, Llama-3.1-Instruct 0.31
Morpheus (authors' attack) is strongest overall: 0.88 GPT-4.1, 0.88 GPT-4.1-mini, 0.80 Grok-3, 0.76 Grok-3-mini, 0.73 OpenAI-o1, 0.40 Claude-3.5; 0.77 Vicuna-7B, 0.58 Qwen-2.5
Notable single ASRs: Past Tense 0.87 and Overload 0.87 on Gemini-2.5; GPTFUZZER 0.89 on Qwen-1.5-Chat; DRA 0.78 and SCAV 0.69 on Vicuna-7B

Evaluator disagreement on Vicuna-7B attack outcomes: PAIR-attack ASR std 0.34 across 19 evaluators; RADAR and ShieldGemma score most attacks far lower (e.g. RADAR Morpheus 0.18, ShieldGemma DeepInception 0.02) than LLM judges (Claude-Sonnet-4 Morpheus 0.99)
Per-category (1-ASR): Claude-3.5 highest robustness across all dimensions; Grok-3 and GPT-4.1-mini weakest in Cybersecurity; open models weakest on synthetic/content-fabrication prompts
baselines_compared: Competing benchmarks positioned against: JailJudge, EasyJailbreak, HarmBench, AISafetyLab, PandaGuard

Attack methods evaluated include TAP, PAIR, RENE, Cipher, Jailbroken, MultiLingual, ArtPrompt, SCAV, ICA, DRA, DeepInception, GPTFUZZER, Past Tense, Overload, Random Search, AutoDAN, LAA, GCG, and Morpheus

Evaluators compared include GPTFuzzer-CLS, HarmBench-CLS, LlamaGuard-3, ShieldLM, ShieldGemma, PatternMatch, PrefixMatch, RADAR, and LLM judges (GPT-5, GPT-4, Claude-Sonnet-4, Gemini, Grok-3, DeepSeek-R1/V3, Doubao, Kimi, Qwen-2.5, Llama-3.1)
Defense methods evaluated on Vicuna-7B include RA-LLM and ICD (best-performing) vs Paraphrasing and Layer-AdvPatcher (limited effectiveness)
